attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman for: security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, multi-step sequences where fragment order risks misread, user asks to clarify or repeats question. Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- ship: Review all uncommitted changes, write a commit message, commit, and push — no co-author line.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- caveman:compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman:compress <filepath> or "compress memory file"
- caveman:caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

6	# Represents a user's login credentials (email + password).
7	#

15	# We use bcrypt (via has_secure_password) which:
16	# - Hashes the password with a random salt
17	# - Uses intentionally slow algorithm (prevents brute-force)
18	# - Automatically handles comparison securely

34	  # - password_confirmation= setter for confirmation validation
35	  # - authenticate(password) method for login verification

file.content:
# frozen_string_literal: true

# =============================================================================
# AUTH CREDENTIAL MODEL
# =============================================================================
# Represents a user's login credentials (email + password).
#
# This is the core model for authentication. It:
# - Stores email and hashed password
# - Validates password requirements (NIST SP 800-63B compliant)
# - Associates with refresh tokens for session management
# - Tracks password history to prevent reuse
#
# SECURITY: Passwords are NEVER stored in plain text.
# We use bcrypt (via has_secure_password) which:
# - Hashes the password with a random salt
# - Uses intentionally slow algorithm (prevents brute-force)
# - Automatically handles comparison securely
class AuthCredential < ApplicationRecord
  # ===========================================================================
  # CONSTANTS
  # ===========================================================================

  # Valid user roles for authorization
  # 'user': Regular patient/customer
  # 'admin': Administrator with elevated privileges
  ROLES = %w[user admin].freeze

  # ===========================================================================
  # SECURE PASSWORD
  # ===========================================================================
  # This Rails macro adds:
  # - password= setter that hashes the password
  # - password_confirmation= setter for confirmation validation
  # - authenticate(password) method for login verification
  # - Validation that password_digest is present
  #
  # Requires 'bcrypt' gem and password_digest column in database
  has_secure_password

  # ===========================================================================
  # ASSOCIATIONS
  # ===========================================================================

  # One user can have multiple refresh tokens (multiple devices/sessions)
  # dependent: :destroy → Delete all tokens when user is deleted
  has_many :refresh_tokens, dependent: :destroy

  # Track password history to prevent reuse (NIST requirement)
  has_many :password_histories, dependent: :destroy

  # Password reset tokens for forgot password flow
  has_many :password_reset_tokens, dependent: :destroy

  # Email verification tokens
  has_many :email_verification_tokens, dependent: :destroy

  # ===========================================================================
  # VALIDATIONS
  # ===========================================================================
  # Validations run before saving to database.
  # Invalid records are not saved, and errors are added to the object.

  # Email must be present, unique, and valid format
  validates :email,
            presence: true,
            uniqueness: { case_sensitive: false },
            format: { with: URI::MailTo::EMAIL_REGEXP }

  # NIST SP 800-63B compliant password validation
  # Only validate when creating or changing password
  # Note: Use :: prefix to reference top-level PasswordValidator (not AuthCredential::PasswordValidator)
  validates_with ::PasswordValidator, if: -> { new_record? || password.present? }

  # Role must be present and one of the valid roles
  validates :role,
            presence: true,
            inclusion: { in: ROLES }

  # ===========================================================================
  # CALLBACKS
  # ===========================================================================

  # Record password in history after successful save (for reuse prevention)
  after_save :record_password_history, if: :saved_change_to_password_digest?

  # ===========================================================================
  # NORMALIZATIONS (Rails 7.1+)
  # ===========================================================================
  # Normalizations transform attribute values before validation/saving.
  # This ensures consistency in the database.

  # Normalize email: strip whitespace and convert to lowercase
  # "  User@Example.COM  " → "user@example.com"
  # Why? Prevents duplicate accounts due to case differences
  normalizes :email, with: ->(email) { email.strip.downcase }

  # ===========================================================================
  # INSTANCE METHODS
  # ===========================================================================

  # ===========================================================================
  # SCOPES
  # ===========================================================================

  scope :active, -> { where(deactivated_at: nil) }
  scope :deactivated, -> { where.not(deactivated_at: nil) }

  # ===========================================================================
  # INSTANCE METHODS
  # ===========================================================================

  # Check if email has been verified
  # Used to restrict certain features to verified users
  def email_verified?
    email_verified_at.present?
  end

  # Check if user is an admin
  # Used for authorization checks
  def admin?
    role == 'admin'
  end

  def deactivated?
    deactivated_at.present?
  end

  def active?
    !deactivated?
  end

  private

  # Record the current password digest in history for reuse prevention
  def record_password_history
    PasswordHistory.record_password(self, password_digest)
  end
end

3	# =============================================================================
4	# AUTH CREDENTIAL FACTORY
5	# =============================================================================
6	# FactoryBot factory for creating test AuthCredential records.
7	#
8	# Usage:
9	#   create(:auth_credential)                    # Basic user
10	#   create(:auth_credential, :admin)            # Admin user
11	#   create(:auth_credential, :verified)         # Email verified user
12	#   create(:auth_credential, email: 'custom@example.com')  # Custom email
13	#   build(:auth_credential)                     # Build without saving
14	
15	FactoryBot.define do
16	  factory :auth_credential do
17	    # Sequence generates unique emails: user1@example.com, user2@example.com, ...
18	    sequence(:email) { |n| "user#{n}@example.com" }
19	
20	    # Default password (meets NIST minimum 14 character requirement)
21	    password { 'securepassword14' }
22	
23	    # Default role
24	    role { 'user' }
25	
26	    # Email not verified by default
27	    email_verified_at { nil }
28	
29	    # =========================================================================
30	    # TRAITS
31	    # =========================================================================
32	    # Traits modify the factory for specific scenarios
33	
34	    # Admin user trait
35	    trait :admin do
36	      role { 'admin' }
37	    end
38	
39	    # Verified email trait
40	    trait :verified do
41	      email_verified_at { 1.day.ago }
42	    end
43	
44	    # Unverified email trait (explicit, for readability)
45	    trait :unverified do
46	      email_verified_at { nil }
47	    end
48	
49	    # Deactivated account trait
50	    trait :deactivated do
51	      deactivated_at { 1.hour.ago }
52	    end
53	  end
54	end
55

file.content:
# frozen_string_literal: true

# =============================================================================
# AUTH CREDENTIAL FACTORY
# =============================================================================
# FactoryBot factory for creating test AuthCredential records.
#
# Usage:
#   create(:auth_credential)                    # Basic user
#   create(:auth_credential, :admin)            # Admin user
#   create(:auth_credential, :verified)         # Email verified user
#   create(:auth_credential, email: 'custom@example.com')  # Custom email
#   build(:auth_credential)                     # Build without saving

FactoryBot.define do
  factory :auth_credential do
    # Sequence generates unique emails: user1@example.com, user2@example.com, ...
    sequence(:email) { |n| "user#{n}@example.com" }

    # Default password (meets NIST minimum 14 character requirement)
    password { 'securepassword14' }

    # Default role
    role { 'user' }

    # Email not verified by default
    email_verified_at { nil }

    # =========================================================================
    # TRAITS
    # =========================================================================
    # Traits modify the factory for specific scenarios

    # Admin user trait
    trait :admin do
      role { 'admin' }
    end

    # Verified email trait
    trait :verified do
      email_verified_at { 1.day.ago }
    end

    # Unverified email trait (explicit, for readability)
    trait :unverified do
      email_verified_at { nil }
    end

    # Deactivated account trait
    trait :deactivated do
      deactivated_at { 1.hour.ago }
    end
  end
end

2	
3	# =============================================================================
4	# PASSWORD VALIDATOR
5	# =============================================================================
6	# NIST SP 800-63B compliant password validation.
7	#
8	# Rules implemented:
9	# - Minimum 14 characters, maximum 64 characters
10	# - All characters allowed (unicode, spaces, symbols)
11	# - No complexity requirements (no forced uppercase, numbers, symbols)
12	# - Must not appear in breached password databases
13	# - Must not be a commonly used password
14	# - Must not match or closely resemble user's email
15	# - Must not reuse last 12 passwords
16	class PasswordValidator < ActiveModel::Validator
17	  MIN_LENGTH = 14
18	  MAX_LENGTH = 64
19	  EMAIL_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.7
20	
21	  def validate(record)
22	    return if record.password.blank?
23	
24	    validate_length(record)
25	    validate_not_common(record)
26	    validate_not_similar_to_email(record)
27	    validate_not_breached(record)
28	    validate_not_reused(record) if password_changing?(record)
29	  end
30	
31	  private
32	
33	  def validate_length(record)
34	    password = record.password
35	
36	    if password.length < MIN_LENGTH
37	      record.errors.add(:password, "must be at least #{MIN_LENGTH} characters")
38	    elsif password.length > MAX_LENGTH
39	      record.errors.add(:password, "must be at most #{MAX_LENGTH} characters")
40	    end
41	  end
42	
43	  def validate_not_common(record)
44	    return unless common_password?(record.password)
45	
46	    record.errors.add(:password, 'is too common and easily guessed')
47	  end
48	
49	  def validate_not_similar_to_email(record)
50	    return if record.email.blank?
51	
52	    email_local_part = record.email.split('@').first&.downcase
53	    password_lower = record.password.downcase
54	
55	    # Check if password contains email or email contains password
56	    if password_lower.include?(email_local_part) || email_local_part.include?(password_lower)
57	      record.errors.add(:password, 'must not be similar to your email address')
58	      return
59	    end
60	
61	    # Check Levenshtein distance for similarity
62	    return unless similar_strings?(password_lower, email_local_part)
63	
64	    record.errors.add(:password, 'must not be similar to your email address')
65	  end
66	
67	  def validate_not_breached(record)
68	    breached = PasswordBreachChecker.breached?(record.password)
69	
70	    # breached is nil if the check failed - we allow the password in that case
71	    # to avoid blocking users due to API issues
72	    return unless breached == true
73	
74	    record.errors.add(:password, 'has been exposed in a data breach and cannot be used')
75	  end
76	
77	  def validate_not_reused(record)
78	    return unless PasswordHistory.password_used?(record, record.password)
79	
80	    record.errors.add(:password, 'cannot be one of your last 12 passwords')
81	  end
82	
83	  def password_changing?(record)
84	    !record.new_record? && record.password_digest_changed?
85	  end
86	
87	  def common_password?(password)
88	    @common_passwords ||= load_common_passwords
89	    @common_passwords.include?(password.downcase)
90	  end
91	
92	  def load_common_passwords
93	    file_path = Rails.root.join('config/common_passwords.txt')
94	    return Set.new unless File.exist?(file_path)
95	
96	    File.readlines(file_path).map(&:strip).to_set(&:downcase)
97	  end
98	
99	  def similar_strings?(str1, str2)
100	    return false if str1.blank? || str2.blank?
101	
102	    distance = StringDistance.levenshtein(str1, str2)
103	    max_length = [str1.length, str2.length].max
104	    similarity = 1.0 - (distance.to_f / max_length)
105	
106	    similarity >= EMAIL_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD
107	  end
108	end
109

file.content:
# frozen_string_literal: true

# =============================================================================
# PASSWORD VALIDATOR
# =============================================================================
# NIST SP 800-63B compliant password validation.
#
# Rules implemented:
# - Minimum 14 characters, maximum 64 characters
# - All characters allowed (unicode, spaces, symbols)
# - No complexity requirements (no forced uppercase, numbers, symbols)
# - Must not appear in breached password databases
# - Must not be a commonly used password
# - Must not match or closely resemble user's email
# - Must not reuse last 12 passwords
class PasswordValidator < ActiveModel::Validator
  MIN_LENGTH = 14
  MAX_LENGTH = 64
  EMAIL_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.7

  def validate(record)
    return if record.password.blank?

    validate_length(record)
    validate_not_common(record)
    validate_not_similar_to_email(record)
    validate_not_breached(record)
    validate_not_reused(record) if password_changing?(record)
  end

  private

  def validate_length(record)
    password = record.password

    if password.length < MIN_LENGTH
      record.errors.add(:password, "must be at least #{MIN_LENGTH} characters")
    elsif password.length > MAX_LENGTH
      record.errors.add(:password, "must be at most #{MAX_LENGTH} characters")
    end
  end

  def validate_not_common(record)
    return unless common_password?(record.password)

    record.errors.add(:password, 'is too common and easily guessed')
  end

  def validate_not_similar_to_email(record)
    return if record.email.blank?

    email_local_part = record.email.split('@').first&.downcase
    password_lower = record.password.downcase

    # Check if password contains email or email contains password
    if password_lower.include?(email_local_part) || email_local_part.include?(password_lower)
      record.errors.add(:password, 'must not be similar to your email address')
      return
    end

    # Check Levenshtein distance for similarity
    return unless similar_strings?(password_lower, email_local_part)

    record.errors.add(:password, 'must not be similar to your email address')
  end

  def validate_not_breached(record)
    breached = PasswordBreachChecker.breached?(record.password)

    # breached is nil if the check failed - we allow the password in that case
    # to avoid blocking users due to API issues
    return unless breached == true

    record.errors.add(:password, 'has been exposed in a data breach and cannot be used')
  end

  def validate_not_reused(record)
    return unless PasswordHistory.password_used?(record, record.password)

    record.errors.add(:password, 'cannot be one of your last 12 passwords')
  end

  def password_changing?(record)
    !record.new_record? && record.password_digest_changed?
  end

  def common_password?(password)
    @common_passwords ||= load_common_passwords
    @common_passwords.include?(password.downcase)
  end

  def load_common_passwords
    file_path = Rails.root.join('config/common_passwords.txt')
    return Set.new unless File.exist?(file_path)

    File.readlines(file_path).map(&:strip).to_set(&:downcase)
  end

  def similar_strings?(str1, str2)
    return false if str1.blank? || str2.blank?

    distance = StringDistance.levenshtein(str1, str2)
    max_length = [str1.length, str2.length].max
    similarity = 1.0 - (distance.to_f / max_length)

    similarity >= EMAIL_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD
  end
end

content.text: Good. Now I'll write the seeds file.

input.content:
# frozen_string_literal: true

# =============================================================================
# DEVELOPMENT SEEDS
# =============================================================================
# Passwords must be >= 14 chars (NIST SP 800-63B).
# Run: docker-compose exec auth-api bundle exec rails db:seed

DEV_PASSWORD = 'devpassword2024'

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Admin account
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
admin = AuthCredential.find_or_initialize_by(email: 'admin@example.com')
admin.assign_attributes(
  password: DEV_PASSWORD,
  role: 'admin',
  email_verified_at: Time.current
)

if admin.save
  puts "Admin created: #{admin.email} / #{DEV_PASSWORD}"
else
  puts "Admin skipped (#{admin.errors.full_messages.join(', ')})"
end

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Regular users
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
30.times do |i|
  email = "user#{i + 1}@example.com"
  user = AuthCredential.find_or_initialize_by(email: email)
  user.assign_attributes(
    password: DEV_PASSWORD,
    role: 'user',
    email_verified_at: Time.current
  )

  if user.save
    print '.'
  else
    puts "\nUser #{email} skipped (#{user.errors.full_messages.join(', ')})"
  end
end

puts "\n30 users seeded."

file.content:
# frozen_string_literal: true

# =============================================================================
# DEVELOPMENT SEEDS
# =============================================================================
# Passwords must be >= 14 chars (NIST SP 800-63B).
# Run: docker-compose exec auth-api bundle exec rails db:seed

DEV_PASSWORD = 'devpassword2024'

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Admin account
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
admin = AuthCredential.find_or_initialize_by(email: 'admin@example.com')
admin.assign_attributes(
  password: DEV_PASSWORD,
  role: 'admin',
  email_verified_at: Time.current
)

if admin.save
  Rails.logger.debug { "Admin created: #{admin.email} / #{DEV_PASSWORD}" }
else
  Rails.logger.debug { "Admin skipped (#{admin.errors.full_messages.join(', ')})" }
end

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Regular users
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
30.times do |i|
  email = "user#{i + 1}@example.com"
  user = AuthCredential.find_or_initialize_by(email: email)
  user.assign_attributes(
    password: DEV_PASSWORD,
    role: 'user',
    email_verified_at: Time.current
  )

  if user.save
    Rails.logger.debug '.'
  else
    Rails.logger.debug { "\nUser #{email} skipped (#{user.errors.full_messages.join(', ')})" }
  end
end

Rails.logger.debug "\n30 users seeded."

content.text:
Formatter swapped `puts` → `Rails.logger.debug`. Fine — output still visible in dev logs. File looks good.

Run with:
```bash
docker-compose exec auth-api bundle exec rails db:seed
```

Credentials:
- **Admin**: `admin@example.com` / `devpassword2024`
- **Users**: `user1@example.com` … `user30@example.com` / `devpassword2024`

`find_or_initialize_by` makes it idempotent — safe to re-run without dupes.
